title: Bounded string processing

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Bounded string processing; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: GNU C Language Introduction and Reference Manual; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Bounded string processing
sources.claim: C string operations require a valid terminated source, a destination with enough capacity, and explicit handling of lengths, truncation, overlap, and conversion errors.

sources.locator: SEI CERT C Coding Standard; living standard accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Bounded string processing
sources.claim: C string operations require a valid terminated source, a destination with enough capacity, and explicit handling of lengths, truncation, overlap, and conversion errors.

canonicalExplanation.summary: C string operations require a valid terminated source, a destination with enough capacity, and explicit handling of lengths, truncation, overlap, and conversion errors.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A bounded line read tracks the array capacity, removes an optional newline, and verifies termination before measuring or copying. Your goal is to implement a bounded string-processing routine and tests for empty, full and malformed inputs. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: while, do and for; Parameters and return values; C string representation; Validated text input. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: C string operations require a valid terminated source, a destination with enough capacity, and explicit handling of lengths, truncation, overlap, and conversion errors. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: strlen excludes the terminator, strncpy may not terminate, concatenation needs remaining capacity, and byte length can differ from displayed character count. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: strncpy always creates a null-terminated string and therefore makes arbitrary source text safe.

examples.title: Bounded string processing: defined case
examples.situation: A bounded line read tracks the array capacity, removes an optional newline, and verifies termination before measuring or copying.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A bounded line read tracks the array capacity, removes an optional newline, and verifies termination before measuring or copying.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Do not assume strncpy terminates; design from destination capacity, validate source bounds, and establish the terminator explicitly when needed.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Do not assume strncpy terminates; design from destination capacity, validate source bounds, and establish the terminator explicitly when needed.

examples.title: Bounded string processing: boundary case
examples.situation: Before appending, code checks that destination length plus source length plus one does not exceed destination capacity.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: Before appending, code checks that destination length plus source length plus one does not exceed destination capacity.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: strlen excludes the terminator, strncpy may not terminate, concatenation needs remaining capacity, and byte length can differ from displayed character count.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: strncpy always creates a null-terminated string and therefore makes arbitrary source text safe.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement a bounded string-processing routine and tests for empty, full and malformed inputs.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: strncpy always creates a null-terminated string and therefore makes arbitrary source text safe.
misconceptions.correction: Do not assume strncpy terminates; design from destination capacity, validate source bounds, and establish the terminator explicitly when needed.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: strncpy always creates a null-terminated string and therefore makes arbitrary source text safe.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Bounded string processing: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
strlen excludes the terminator, strncpy may not terminate, concatenation needs remaining capacity, and byte length can differ from displayed character count.
